title: Checkerboard Artifacts
concept: Transposed convolutions can create grid-like checkerboard patterns in their output because overlapping regions aren't summed evenly across the whole image; smoother alternatives like upsampling followed by a regular convolution avoid the problem.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a transposed convolution, what happens where two spread-out patches overlap in the output?

options.text: Both values are averaged and halved

exercises.explain: Right — overlaps sum. That summing has a side effect worth knowing about.

cards.title: A strange grid pattern shows up
cards.body: Zoom into images generated by early transposed-convolution decoders — GANs, segmentation masks, super-resolution outputs — and you'll often spot a faint grid of light and dark squares laid over everything, like a checkerboard. That's not a rendering bug. It comes straight from the math of the transposed convolution itself.

cards.title: What causes checkerboard artifacts?
cards.body: Checkerboard artifacts happen when a transposed convolution's stride and filter size don't divide evenly, so some output positions get contributions from many overlapping patches while their neighbors get contributions from only one or two. Those uneven overlap counts show up visually as a repeating grid pattern.

cards.title: Uneven overlap in practice
cards.body: Picture the tiny [3, 11, 10] example from the last lesson: position 1 summed two overlapping contributions, while positions 0 and 2 each got only one. Now imagine that same unevenness repeating across a whole 2D image, thousands of times — every 'position 1' spot ends up brighter or darker than its neighbors, tiling into a visible checkerboard.

cards.title: Uneven paint coats
cards.body: Imagine painting a fence by pressing a sponge along it, but the presses overlap unevenly — some fence boards get two coats, others get only one. Even with perfect paint and perfect technique, you'd still see stripes of uneven color. That's checkerboarding: the overlap pattern itself is uneven, no matter how well the 'painter' (the learned weights) is trained.

cards.title: Not a training bug you can just fix with more data
cards.body: It's tempting to think checkerboard artifacts mean the network needs more training data or more epochs. They don't — the pattern is baked into the geometry of how the transposed convolution's overlaps land, so more training alone won't erase it. The fix has to change the operation itself.

cards.title: Transposed conv vs. a smoother alternative
cards.body: One route risks uneven overlap; the other sidesteps it:

left.text:
Learned weights, but stride/filter
overlap unevenly →
checkerboard risk

right.text:
Simple upsample first,
then a normal conv smooths it —
no uneven overlap

exercises.prompt: What is the root cause of checkerboard artifacts in transposed convolution output?

options.text: Uneven overlap between spread-out patches, caused by the stride and filter size not dividing evenly
Running the network on grayscale instead of color images

exercises.explain: Right — it's a geometric side effect of how the patches overlap, not a data problem.

exercises.prompt: True or false: checkerboard artifacts can be fully fixed just by training the network for more epochs.
exercises.explain: False — the pattern is baked into the overlap geometry, so more training alone doesn't remove it.

exercises.sentence: A common alternative that avoids checkerboard artifacts is to upsample with a simple fixed rule first, then smooth the result with a regular ___.
exercises.explain: Right — upsample first, then a regular convolution smooths out the result without uneven overlap.

exercises.prompt: Which fix best addresses checkerboard artifacts at their root cause?

options.text: Replacing the transposed convolution with a simple upsample step followed by a regular convolution
Lowering the learning rate during training
Adding more color channels to the input image

exercises.explain: Exactly — changing the operation itself fixes the geometry problem; tuning training doesn't.

exercises.prompt: A team's image generator produces outputs with a faint repeating grid pattern. They try training for 10x longer, but the pattern doesn't go away. What does this tell them about the cause, and what's a proven fix?

options.text: The pattern is caused by uneven overlap in the transposed convolution's geometry, not undertraining — switching to upsample-then-regular-conv is a proven fix
The network needs even more training data before the pattern will disappear
The pattern means the images are actually generating correctly and nothing should change
The fix is to remove all convolutions from the decoder entirely

exercises.explain: Exactly — since more training didn't help, the cause is geometric, not a learning problem. Swapping in upsample + regular conv sidesteps the uneven overlap entirely. Coming up next: where transposed convolutions actually get used in real systems.
